role_summary: Port Gibson, Mississippi NAACP organizer; canvasser for voting rights and Black-business-boycott participant in the Port Gibson Merchant Boycotts; participant in the NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware Co. trial that produced the 1982 Supreme Court decision affirming the First Amendment protection of civil-rights economic boycotts.
ai_reading: Guster's top semantic neighbor is Peggy Jean Connor (entry 96) at a cosine count of 112; the broader cluster includes Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones (entry 92, Claiborne County / Port Gibson Movement organizer), Charles McLaurin (entry 17, Mississippi SNCC), and the joint Carolyn Miller / James Miller interview (entry 12, also Port Gibson NAACP youth organizers). The Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones and Miller cosines are the catalog's clearest Port-Gibson-and-Claiborne-County NAACP-organizing-tradition signature: all four pages document the same boycott-and-protest arc that produced the 1982 NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware Supreme Court decision. The novel observation her page surfaces is the church-and-voter-rights-canvasser register: Guster's oral history specifically discusses the role of Black churches in the Civil Rights Movement and her own canvassing work for voting rights, the catalog's clearest documentation of church-mediated voter-registration organizing in the Port Gibson context.
biographical_paragraph: While Guster's LoC oral history carries a publication-block audit tier (known issues prevent full publication-grade certification, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external sources before citation), the external biographical record is documented through the Civil Rights History Project finding aid for entry 77 [src: 1]. Guster grew up in Port Gibson, Mississippi and Chicago, Illinois, experiencing segregation in both cities. She returned to Port Gibson and became active in the local civil rights movement: she canvassed for voting rights, joined the Port Gibson Merchant Boycotts in support of Black-business interests, and joined the Port Gibson NAACP. She participated in the trial NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware Co. (1982), the Supreme Court decision affirming the First Amendment protection of civil-rights economic boycotts that emerged from the Port Gibson organizing arc she contributed to [src: 1].

interview_snippets.speaker: Leesco Guster
interview_snippets.lead_in: Guster describes what drew her into the Claiborne County movement before she had joined the NAACP.
interview_snippets.quote: My thing was, I wanted to vote. I wanted to get people registered to vote. I wanted a little recognition and a better job. I may not get it, but somebody else come along.

interview_snippets.speaker: Leesco Guster
interview_snippets.lead_in: Asked whether she hesitated about hosting movement workers and doing voter registration in the 1960s, Guster answers.
interview_snippets.quote: I don't know where it came from, but I was brave.

interview_snippets.speaker: Leesco Guster
interview_snippets.lead_in: On the difficulty of canvassing tenant farmers for voter registration in rural Claiborne County.
interview_snippets.quote: We tried. But mostly the people who live on plantations, they were afraid.

interview_snippets.speaker: Leesco Guster
interview_snippets.lead_in: Guster describes the work she did with the youth group that Rudy Shields organized and placed under her.
interview_snippets.quote: They were the young people singing, you know, the freedom songs and stuff like that. And then I even took them to church several times and let the choir sign.

interview_snippets.speaker: Leesco Guster
interview_snippets.lead_in: Asked what advice she would give young people based on what she learned in the movement.
interview_snippets.quote: When you speak up for yourself but you can't just sit there and talk, you got to work.

interview_snippets.speaker: Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones
interview_snippets.lead_in: Fellow Claiborne County NAACP organizer Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones (entry 92) answers when the interviewer asks what Leesco Guster was like.
interview_snippets.quote: Oh, she just went with Rudy Shields, and she did most of what was Rudy Shields, Ms. Leesco Guster Walker.

movement_context: Port Gibson sits in Claiborne County, a majority-Black rural county in southwestern Mississippi where the local NAACP branch was founded in the 1940s by Ernest Jones and rebuilt in the early 1950s through a word-of-mouth recruitment campaign that operated under the constant threat of economic retaliation [src: 2]. The branch that Guster joined traces directly to that earlier organizing tradition. By late 1965 or early 1966 local activists, with assistance from Mississippi NAACP field secretary Charles Evers, reorganized the Claiborne County branch and elected James Dorsey, pastor of First Baptist Church, as its president [src: 3]. In March 1966 the branch presented white elected officials with a list of demands covering desegregation of public accommodations under the Civil Rights Act of 1964, the hiring of Black police officers and sheriff's deputies, the addition of Black residents to community boards and juries, and the extension of courtesy titles such as Mr. and Mrs. to African Americans [src: 3]. When officials declined to negotiate, a mass meeting at First Baptist Church on April 1, 1966 voted unanimously to launch a boycott of white-owned merchants in Port Gibson and Claiborne County, and Charles Evers led more than 450 protesters from the church to the county courthouse to announce it [src: 3]. The boycott combined nonviolent picketing with sustained voter-registration canvassing, the work Guster's interview describes from her own participation. Women carried much of the organizing weight: four of every five newly registered voters in Claiborne County during the boycott years were women, and women attended mass meetings and picketed at higher rates than men [src: 4]. Black churches were the movement's institutional spine, First Baptist Church hosted the founding boycott meeting and continued to host mass meetings structured as services centered on freedom themes, providing venues for singing, preaching, and strategy [src: 4]. Historian Emilye Crosby's community study of Claiborne County argues that the legal victories of the mid-1960s did not end the movement in places like Port Gibson but rather emboldened local organizers to press new demands around local issues, with armed self-defense and women's leadership both more present than the national-movement narrative recognizes [src: 5]. The boycott continued for years and drew a 1969 damages suit from white merchants that the Mississippi Supreme Court eventually decided against the NAACP, holding 92 individual petitioners liable for the entire economic consequences of the action [src: 6].
legacy_and_reception: The legal arc that grew from the Port Gibson boycott reached its conclusion on July 3, 1982, when the United States Supreme Court reversed the Mississippi judgment in NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware Co., 458 U.S. 886 [src: 6]. Justice John Paul Stevens wrote for a unanimous Court that the nonviolent elements of the boycott, the speeches, picketing, and economic pressure aimed at securing constitutional and civil rights, were protected expressive activity under the First Amendment, and that participants could not be held liable in damages for the consequences of that protected conduct [src: 6]. The decision established the constitutional protection of politically motivated economic boycotts and remains a foundational citation in First Amendment doctrine on collective expressive action [src: 7]. The Port Gibson community marked the victory with a public Victory Day celebration on July 29, 1982, featuring marches that retraced 1960s protest routes and gathering remarks from national NAACP leadership [src: 4]. The case sits at the center of historian Emilye Crosby's book-length study of the Claiborne County movement, which received the McLemore Prize from the Mississippi Historical Society and an honorable mention for the Organization of American Historians' Liberty Legacy Foundation Award [src: 5]. Within the Civil Rights History Project collection at the Library of Congress, Guster's oral history is catalogued alongside interviews with other Port Gibson and Claiborne County organizers from the same arc, including Nathaniel Hawthorne Jones and the joint Carolyn Miller / James Miller interview, providing a multi-voice archival record of how the boycott was sustained at the household and neighborhood level over more than a decade [src: 1].

gallery.alt: A church in Port Gibson, Mississippi, photographed in 1940
gallery.caption: A church in Port Gibson, Mississippi, August 1940. Guster's oral history describes the role of Black churches in the local movement, and First Baptist Church hosted the April 1, 1966 mass meeting that voted to launch the merchant boycott she joined.
gallery.repository: Farm Security Administration collection, Library of Congress
gallery.license: Public domain (work of the U.S. Farm Security Administration)

gallery.alt: A street scene with storefronts in downtown Port Gibson, Mississippi, photographed in 1940
gallery.caption: A street scene in downtown Port Gibson, Mississippi, August 1940, showing storefronts along the commercial district. This merchant corridor is the kind of white-owned business the 1966 Port Gibson Merchant Boycotts targeted, the boycott Guster participated in.
gallery.repository: Farm Security Administration collection, Library of Congress
gallery.license: Public domain (work of the U.S. Farm Security Administration)

gallery.alt: The Claiborne County Courthouse in Port Gibson, Mississippi, photographed in 2008
gallery.caption: The Claiborne County Courthouse in Port Gibson, Mississippi, 2008. On April 1, 1966, Charles Evers led more than 450 boycott participants from First Baptist Church to this courthouse to announce the action that became NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware Co., the case Guster participated in.
